title: Adding headbands by hand
subtitle: Sewing a silk-and-core headband at the spine head and tail
excerpt: Headbands are the small banded structures at the top and bottom of a book's spine. In machine-made books they are glued-on strips of cotton tape; in a hand-sewn binding they are worked stitch by stitch over a rolled core, locking each signature to the spine and adding colour.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on headbands. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Cockerell describes the two-colour chain-and-spine stitch headband used here.

content.text: is one of the most satisfying details in bookbinding: visible, structural, and the clearest visual marker that a book was made by hand. This tutorial works a two-colour chain stitch in linen thread over a cotton-tape core. The spine stitches link the headband to the sewn text block at every second signature, giving the head and tail of the spine a firm, tight finish.

items.qty: Two lengths, each 20 mm longer than the spine width
items.name: Cotton tape (headband form or rolled paper)

items.qty: Approx. 60 cm per colour per headband
items.name: Waxed linen bookbinding thread, 4-cord, in two contrasting colours

items.qty: For drawing thread across if not pre-waxed
items.name: Beeswax block

attrs.heading: Materials for two headbands (head and tail)

content.text: Position the sewn text block on its spine with the head facing you. Hold the headband core (a length of cotton tape or rolled paper) horizontally at the head of the spine, flush with the back edge of the spine and projecting 10 mm beyond the foredge. Tie on the first thread colour with a reef knot around the core at the foredge projection.
Work the chain stitch over the core

Pass the needle behind the core from front to back, bring it forward through the loop of thread from the previous stitch (the chain-link), then pull snug. Each stitch encircles the core once. Pack the stitches tight using the bone folder edge after every fourth stitch so no core colour shows between them. Change colour every two stitches for a narrow two-colour stripe, or every six stitches for blocks.
At every second or third signature, work a

: before encircling the core for the next chain stitch, pass the needle down through the spine between the two nearest signatures (the sewing thread is visible at the spine if you look sideways along it). Draw through, return up through the same gap, then continue the chain stitch over the core as normal. This links the headband structurally to the text block.

At the spine edge, work one extra spine stitch anchored firmly in the spine. Tie off both thread colours with a small, neat knot around the back of the last chain stitch. Trim the core projections to 5 mm; tuck them neatly under the spine cloth or trim flush once the book is cased in. Repeat at the tail.

Headbands fit any case-bound structure. They are most often worked before the text block is glued in; the spine stitch threads through the same sewing gaps used during text-block sewing. Add headbands to the quarter-bound book or to any coptic-stitch hardback for the finishing detail that marks the piece as fully hand-bound.
